pdg_node.statement: (points: array-like of shape (N, D) — N points in D-dimensional space, incremental: bool — whether to allow incremental addition of points, qhull_options: str — additional options to pass to Qhull) -> scipy.spatial.Voronoi — object containing vertices, ridge_points, ridge_vertices, regions, point_region
pdg_node.informal_desc: Computes the Voronoi diagram for a set of input points using Qhull, partitioning space into regions closest to each point. Supports incremental construction and custom Qhull options.

declaration.name: voronoitessellation
declaration.type_signature: (points: array-like of shape (N, D) — N points in D-dimensional space, incremental: bool — whether to allow incremental addition of points, qhull_options: str — additional options to pass to Qhull) -> scipy.spatial.Voronoi — object containing vertices, ridge_points, ridge_vertices, regions, point_region
declaration.docstring: Computes the Voronoi diagram for a set of input points using Qhull, partitioning space into regions closest to each point. Supports incremental construction and custom Qhull options.
declaration.conceptual_summary: ProximityPartitionedDualDecomposition. Given a finite set of N anchor points embedded in a D-dimensional metric space, this operation partitions the entire ambient space into N convex polyhedral cells such that every location within a cell is strictly closer (under Euclidean distance) to that cell's anchor than to any other anchor — a nearest-neighbor dual tiling. The output encodes the full combinatorial topology of this partition: the shared boundary facets (ridges) between adjacent cells, the coordinates of boundary intersection vertices, and the mapping from each anchor to its enclosing region, with unbounded cells flagged via a sentinel index.. Applications: Computational neuroscience — receptive field modeling: partition sensory stimulus space into response territories for a population of neurons, where each anchor is a neuron's preferred stimulus vector; ridge boundaries define decision thresholds between competing neural representations, Wireless network engineering — coverage zone allocation: given N base-station coordinates in 2D/3D space, partition the service area into association zones where each mobile device is assigned to its geometrically nearest transmitter, with ridge facets defining optimal handoff boundaries, Crystallography and materials science — grain boundary analysis: model polycrystalline microstructure by treating nucleation seed coordinates as anchors; the resulting polyhedral cells represent idealized crystal grains and their shared ridges encode grain-boundary interfaces for mechanical stress simulation, Unsupervised machine learning — cluster boundary geometry: given cluster centroid vectors in a high-dimensional feature space, construct the exact geometric decision boundaries of a nearest-centroid classifier, enabling visualization of cluster overlap, boundary complexity, and outlier exposure without requiring label data

pdg_node.statement: (points: array-like of shape (N, D) — N points in D-dimensional space, incremental: bool — whether to allow incremental addition of points, qhull_options: str — additional options to pass to Qhull) -> scipy.spatial.Delaunay — object containing simplices, neighbors, equations, coplanar
pdg_node.informal_desc: Computes the Delaunay triangulation for a set of input points using Qhull, producing a simplex mesh where no point lies inside the circumsphere of any simplex. Supports incremental construction and custom Qhull options.

declaration.name: delaunaytriangulation
declaration.type_signature: (points: array-like of shape (N, D) — N points in D-dimensional space, incremental: bool — whether to allow incremental addition of points, qhull_options: str — additional options to pass to Qhull) -> scipy.spatial.Delaunay — object containing simplices, neighbors, equations, coplanar
declaration.docstring: Computes the Delaunay triangulation for a set of input points using Qhull, producing a simplex mesh where no point lies inside the circumsphere of any simplex. Supports incremental construction and custom Qhull options.
declaration.conceptual_summary: MaximalEmptyCircumsphere Simplex Mesh Constructor. Given an unordered set of N points embedded in D-dimensional Euclidean space, partitions the convex hull of the point set into a maximal collection of non-overlapping D-simplices (triangles in 2D, tetrahedra in 3D, etc.) such that the open circumsphere of every simplex contains no other input point. This circumsphere-emptiness constraint uniquely defines the tessellation (up to degeneracy) and simultaneously encodes the dual Voronoi diagram — the perpendicular bisector partition of space into nearest-neighbor regions. The construction supports incremental point insertion, allowing the mesh topology to be updated as new points are streamed in without full recomputation.. Applications: Computational Neuroscience — Reconstructing 3D dendritic arbor topology from sparse electrode-recorded neuron soma coordinates: the simplex mesh defines anatomically plausible connectivity skeletons and nearest-neighbor synaptic coupling regions without any domain assumptions about branching patterns., Geophysical Seismology — Interpolating subsurface wave-velocity scalars across irregularly distributed seismograph stations: each 3-simplex (tetrahedron) defines a locally linear interpolation cell, and the dual Voronoi cells define each station's zone of influence for tomographic inversion., Robotics & Motion Planning — Constructing a collision-free configuration-space roadmap from a stochastically sampled set of N-dimensional joint-angle vectors: Delaunay edges connect configuration-space neighbors with maximally 'fat' cells, reducing narrow-passage artifacts common in grid-based planners., Materials Science / Molecular Dynamics — Identifying structural defects (vacancies, interstitials) in crystalline lattices by triangulating atomic position snapshots: deviations from the ideal simplex geometry (circumradius anomalies) directly flag lattice distortions, dislocations, and amorphous regions without reference to a pre-defined unit cell.
